Earth's oceans hold about 97 percent of all the planet's water, yet we have mapped more of Mars than our own seafloor.
The average depth of the ocean is around 3,700 meters, deep enough to sink most mountains without a trace.
At the bottom of the Mariana Trench the pressure is over 1,000 times that at the surface, like a car pressing on your thumbnail.
Challenger Deep is nearly 11,000 meters down, deeper than Mount Everest is tall by well over two kilometers.
More people have flown to space than have descended to the deepest point of the ocean.
Sunlight essentially vanishes below about 1,000 meters, leaving most of the ocean in permanent darkness.
About 90 percent of the ocean's living space is in the deep, cold, dark waters below the sunlit surface.
Bioluminescence is so common in the deep sea that most animals there make their own light.
The anglerfish dangles a glowing lure grown from its own dorsal fin to draw prey into its jaws.
A blue whale's heart is roughly the size of a small car, and its beat can be detected from several kilometers away.
The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have lived, larger than any dinosaur.
Blue whale calls can travel hundreds of kilometers underwater because sound moves far faster and farther in water than in air.
Sound travels about four times faster in seawater than in air, near 1,500 meters per second.
Colossal squid have the largest eyes of any known animal, each about the size of a dinner plate.
A single giant squid tentacle can stretch longer than a school bus.
The Greenland shark can live for centuries, with some individuals estimated to be over 250 years old.
Greenland sharks may not reach sexual maturity until they are around 150 years old.
One quahog clam named Ming was about 507 years old, born before Columbus reached the Americas.
The immortal jellyfish can revert to an earlier life stage, potentially escaping death by aging.
Deep-sea gigantism causes many creatures like isopods and squid to grow far larger than their shallow-water relatives.
The giant isopod looks like a woodlouse the size of a house cat and can survive years without eating.
Hydrothermal vents were only discovered in 1977, revealing entire ecosystems that need no sunlight at all.
Vent ecosystems run on chemosynthesis, where bacteria turn chemicals like hydrogen sulfide into food.
Water gushing from black smoker vents can exceed 400 degrees Celsius yet does not boil due to crushing pressure.
Giant tube worms near vents have no mouth or gut and rely on bacteria living inside them for food.
Tube worms can grow over two meters long in the total darkness of the deep-sea floor.
The Pompeii worm thrives in vent water hot enough to cook most animals, among the most heat-tolerant creatures known.
Yeti crabs farm bacteria on their hairy claws near hydrothermal vents and appear to wave them to help the bacteria grow.
The barreleye fish has a transparent head, letting it look straight up through its own skull to spot prey.
Some deep-sea fish are jet black, absorbing over 99.5 percent of light to become nearly invisible.
The vampire squid's name means vampire squid from hell, but it feeds harmlessly on marine snow drifting down.
Marine snow is a constant drizzle of dead plankton and debris that feeds much of the deep-sea food web.
The gulper eel can unhinge its jaw to swallow prey larger than itself, its mouth ballooning open.
Fangtooth fish have teeth so long relative to their body they cannot fully close their mouths.
Some deep-sea dragonfish emit red light that their prey cannot see, giving them a private searchlight.
Cookiecutter sharks gouge round plugs of flesh from whales, seals, and even submarines' sonar domes.
The Portuguese man o' war is not one animal but a colony of specialized organisms living together.
A group of siphonophores can form colonies over 40 meters long, rivaling the blue whale in length.
The RMS Titanic wreck lies about 3,800 meters down and was not found until 1985, decades after it sank.
Rust-eating bacteria are slowly consuming the Titanic, forming icicle-like structures called rusticles.
Scientists estimate the Titanic's hull could largely collapse within decades as microbes devour the steel.
There are an estimated three million shipwrecks scattered across the world's ocean floors.
The oldest known intact shipwreck is a Greek trading vessel over 2,400 years old, found in the Black Sea.
Low oxygen in the deep Black Sea preserves ancient wooden shipwrecks almost perfectly for millennia.
The Antikythera wreck yielded an ancient Greek analog computer that predicted astronomical positions.
Coral reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor but host about a quarter of all marine species.
The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth and is visible from low Earth orbit.
Coral animals build reefs by secreting limestone skeletons, one tiny polyp at a time over thousands of years.
Some deep-sea corals grow in cold, dark water and can live for thousands of years.
Black coral colonies have been found that are over 4,000 years old, among the oldest living things on Earth.
The ocean produces at least half of the oxygen we breathe, most of it from tiny drifting plankton.
A single genus of ocean bacteria, Prochlorococcus, may be the most abundant photosynthetic organism on Earth.
Phytoplankton are so numerous that ocean blooms can be seen from space as swirls of turquoise and green.
Krill in Antarctic waters may collectively outweigh the entire human population.
The largest known animal migration on Earth happens every night as deep-sea creatures rise to feed near the surface.
This nightly vertical migration is so massive that ships' sonar once mistook it for a false seafloor.
Sperm whales can dive over 2,000 meters and hold their breath for around 90 minutes hunting squid.
Sperm whales produce the loudest sound of any animal, clicks that can exceed 230 decibels underwater.
Cuvier's beaked whale holds the mammal diving record, plunging nearly 3,000 meters on a single breath.
One tracked Cuvier's beaked whale stayed underwater for over three hours and forty minutes.
The Weddell seal can dive under Antarctic ice for over an hour and navigate back to breathing holes in the dark.
Emperor penguins can dive over 500 meters deep and stay under for more than 20 minutes.
Leatherback turtles dive over 1,000 meters and are one of the deepest-diving air-breathing reptiles.
Sea otters have the densest fur of any animal, up to a million hairs per square inch, instead of blubber.
Mantis shrimp have up to 16 types of color receptors, compared with just three in human eyes.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue, copper-based blood instead of red iron-based blood.
An octopus can taste with its arms, each lined with suckers full of chemical sensors.
The mimic octopus can impersonate the shapes and movements of sea snakes, lionfish, and flatfish.
The dumbo octopus lives thousands of meters deep and flaps ear-like fins to swim through the abyss.
Some deep-sea octopuses brood their eggs for over four years, the longest known egg-guarding in any animal.
Electric eels can generate jolts of up to 860 volts to stun prey and defend themselves.
Electric eels are not true eels but a type of knifefish more closely related to catfish.
The sunfish, or mola mola, is the heaviest bony fish, weighing as much as a small car.
A single ocean sunfish can release up to 300 million eggs at once, more than almost any other vertebrate.
The oarfish can grow over eight meters long and inspired many ancient sea serpent legends.
Whale sharks are the largest fish in the sea, yet they feed almost entirely on tiny plankton.
Each whale shark has a unique pattern of spots, like a fingerprint, used to identify individuals.
Sharks are older than trees, having existed for over 400 million years, long before the first forests appeared on land.
Some shark species must keep swimming to breathe, forcing water over their gills as they move.
The tongue of a blue whale can weigh as much as an entire elephant.
A narwhal's tusk is actually an enormous spiraled tooth packed with millions of nerve endings.
The narwhal's tusk can grow up to three meters long and is usually found only on males.
Beluga whales can change the shape of their bulbous forehead, called a melon, to focus their sounds.
The ocean's largest waterfall is entirely underwater, a cold cascade in the Denmark Strait taller than any land falls.
The Mid-Ocean Ridge is the longest mountain range on Earth, stretching over 65,000 kilometers underwater.
Most of the planet's volcanic activity happens unseen along mid-ocean ridges beneath the waves.
There may be more than a million undiscovered underwater volcanoes, called seamounts, across the ocean floor.
Brinicles are underwater icicles of freezing brine that can kill sea stars and urchins on the seabed they reach.
The deepest fish ever filmed was a snailfish recorded over 8,300 meters down in a Pacific trench.
Below about 8,400 meters, water pressure likely makes it impossible for any fish to survive.
Xenophyophores are single-celled organisms in the deep sea that can grow up to 20 centimeters wide.
Sea cucumbers can eject their internal organs to distract predators, then regrow them later.
Some sea cucumbers breathe through their rear ends, drawing water in and out to absorb oxygen.
The abyssal plains covering much of the deep ocean floor are among the flattest surfaces on Earth.
Manganese nodules the size of potatoes carpet parts of the deep seabed and take millions of years to form.
The ocean absorbs roughly a quarter of the carbon dioxide humans release into the atmosphere.
Seawater is salty largely because rivers carry dissolved minerals from rocks into the ocean over eons.
If all the salt in the ocean were spread over land, it would form a layer about 150 meters thick.
The Pacific Ocean is so vast it could hold all the continents with room to spare.
About 80 percent of the ocean remains unexplored, unmapped, and unseen by human eyes.
Deep-sea brine pools are lakes of super-salty water that sit on the seafloor with their own shorelines and waves.
Falling whale carcasses, called whale falls, can feed deep-sea ecosystems for decades after the whale dies.
